comment_text.sn8.2:1.1: MS edition inserts a … pe… here indicating an abbreviation, apparently by mistake. This is the only occurrence of this pattern in the Suttas.
comment_text.sn8.2:3.2: “Planet” is jagatī; both words stem from the celestial bodies who “wander” about the sky. “Planet’s deep” (jagatogadha) is a poetic term for things underground, a favorite place for hiding treasure (MN 128:20.5, Kp 8).
comment_text.sn8.2:3.4: Muta is the past participle of maññati, “to think, to examine”; it recurs in the next verse. The unique term mutattā (“the self-examined”) recalls Yājñavalkya, “when the Self is seen, heard, thought (or examined), cognized” (ātmani khalv are dṛṣṭe śrute mate vijñāta, Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 4.5.6).
comment_text.sn8.2:4.2: Here paṭigha is added to the regular set diṭṭha, suta, and muta.  I take the line as divided by ca, so that the noun paṭigha is qualified by the two past participles in the first clause. Paṭigha has its regular meaning of any kind of “impingement” through the physical senses (DN 15:20.4, MN 77:22.8, SN 1.23:4.3). Muta is distinct, as “thought” is experienced via “labeling” (DN 15:20.2), not by “impingement”.
comment_text.sn8.2:5.1: Presumably an abbreviated reference to the sixty-two views of the Brahmajālasutta in which the unwary may be trapped (DN 1:3.72.2; referenced as “sixty-three” at Snp 3.6:42.1).

root_text.sn8.2:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ …pe… āyasmā vaṅgīso āḷaviyaṁ viharati aggāḷave cetiye āyasmatā nigrodhakappena upajjhāyena saddhiṁ.
root_text.sn8.2:1.2: Tena kho pana samayena āyasmā nigrodhakappo pacchābhattaṁ piṇḍapātapaṭikkanto vihāraṁ pavisati, sāyaṁ vā nikkhamati aparajju vā kāle.
root_text.sn8.2:1.3: Tena kho pana samayena āyasmato vaṅgīsassa anabhirati uppannā hoti, rāgo cittaṁ anuddhaṁseti.
root_text.sn8.2:1.5: “alābhā vata me, na vata me lābhā; dulladdhaṁ vata me, na vata me suladdhaṁ;
root_text.sn8.2:1.6: yassa me anabhirati uppannā, rāgo cittaṁ anuddhaṁseti; taṁ kutettha labbhā, yaṁ me paro anabhiratiṁ vinodetvā abhiratiṁ uppādeyya.
root_text.sn8.2:1.7: Yannūnāhaṁ attanāva attano anabhiratiṁ vinodetvā abhiratiṁ uppādeyyan”ti.
root_text.sn8.2:1.8: Atha kho āyasmā vaṅgīso attanāva attano anabhiratiṁ vinodetvā abhiratiṁ uppādetvā tāyaṁ velāyaṁ imā gāthāyo abhāsi:
root_text.sn8.2:4.4: Yo ettha na limpati taṁ munimāhu.

translation_text.sn8.2:1.1: At one time Venerable Vaṅgīsa was staying near Āḷavī, at Āḷavī’s premier shrine, together with his mentor, Venerable Nigrodhakappa.
translation_text.sn8.2:1.2: Now at that time after Venerable Nigrodhakappa had finished his meal, on his return from almsround, he would enter his dwelling and not emerge for the rest of that day, or the next.
translation_text.sn8.2:1.3: And at that time Venerable Vaṅgīsa became dissatisfied, as lust infected his mind.
translation_text.sn8.2:1.6: that I’ve become dissatisfied, with lust infecting my mind. How is it possible for someone else to dispel my discontent and give rise to satisfaction?
translation_text.sn8.2:1.7: Why don’t I do it myself?”
translation_text.sn8.2:1.8: Then, on the occasion of dispelling his own discontent and giving rise to satisfaction, he recited these verses:
translation_text.sn8.2:2.2: along with all thoughts of domestic life,
translation_text.sn8.2:2.3: they wouldn’t get snarled in anything;
translation_text.sn8.2:2.4: unsnarled, undesiring: that’s a real mendicant.
translation_text.sn8.2:3.1: Whatever here is included in form—
translation_text.sn8.2:3.2: on earth, in the sky, or in planet’s deep—
translation_text.sn8.2:3.3: wears out, it is all impermanent:
translation_text.sn8.2:3.4: the self-examined live having comprehended this.
translation_text.sn8.2:4.1: People are bound to their attachments,
translation_text.sn8.2:4.2: to impingement of the seen and heard, <j>and to what is thought.
translation_text.sn8.2:4.3: Unstirred, dispel desire for these things;
translation_text.sn8.2:4.4: for one called ‘a sage’ does not cling to them.
translation_text.sn8.2:5.1: Trapped in the sixty wrong views, <j>full of their own opinions,
translation_text.sn8.2:5.2: ordinary people are fixed in wrong principles.
translation_text.sn8.2:5.3: But that mendicant wouldn’t join a sect,
translation_text.sn8.2:5.4: still less would they utter lewd speech.
translation_text.sn8.2:6.1: Clever, serene for a long time,
translation_text.sn8.2:6.2: free of deceit, alert, without envy,
translation_text.sn8.2:6.3: the sage has reached the state of peace;

reference_text.sn8.2:1.1: csp1ed12.188, csp2ed12.188, ms12S1_1306, msdiv210, ndp12.186, pts-vp-pli2ed1.401, sya15.274
